text: The surgeon performs the hernia repair using a three-port technique, inserting a twelve millimeter trocar in the supraumbilical space and two eight millimeter robotic trocars in the left and right upper quadrants.
The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg, with the robotic system docked over the right shoulder, preparing for the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon begins the hernia repair procedure by reducing the hernia content, visually repositioning protruding tissue back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon dissects a preperitoneal plane caudal to the hernia defect, similar to the technique used in tap inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon approaches the hernia sac, with the xiphoid process visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon reduces the hernia sac, carefully manipulating the tissue to reposition it within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon continues to reduce the hernia sac and preperitoneal content, revealing that the hernia defect is larger than initially observed.
The surgeon transects the hernia sac and continues to reduce the preperitoneal fat, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
Following the complete reduction of the hernia sac, the surgeon performs a wide dissection of the preperitoneal flap, expanding the surgical field around the hernia defect.
The surgeon places a large overlapping sheet of mesh over the hernia defect to reinforce the repair, ensuring coverage of the previously reduced preperitoneal content.
The surgeon uses O-ethabon suture in an interrupted fashion to primarily close the hernia defect, similar to the technique used for primary closure of the hiatus.
The surgeon places sutures on the xiphoid process, diaphragm, and the periosteum of the overlying ribs, securing these anatomical structures as part of the hernia repair procedure.
A 15x15 cm sheet of Prolene soft mesh is cut to size and positioned to reinforce the primary closure of the hernia repair.
The surgeon demonstrates the handling of a large sheet of mesh within the preperitoneal space, showing its flexibility and ease of manipulation during placement.
The surgeon uses a secure strap to fixate the mesh at cardinal points along the palpable abdomen, ensuring stable placement of the reinforcement material.
The surgeon uses partial thickness sutures to fixate the mesh along the diaphragm, ensuring secure attachment within the surgical field.
Sutures are placed on the central tendon of the diaphragm to secure the mesh in position.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum using a secure strap, ensuring the mesh is properly fixated within the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon uses the hernia sac to cover areas of exposed mesh within the preperitoneal space, ensuring complete coverage and integration with surrounding tissue.
